summary: Gruber argues that Apple's base-model RAM in Macs has stagnated for over a decade, a problem now made more conspicuous by the demands of on-device AI features. He notes that iMacs have been stuck at 8 GB since 2012 and MacBook Airs since 2017, breaking Apple's historical pattern of regular RAM increases. While acknowledging that Apple silicon's memory efficiency may effectively double the usable performance per GB, he contends that more RAM is always better and more future-proof. The absurdity is underscored by the fact that all iPhone 16 models will match base-model Macs at 8 GB of RAM.
key_points: Base-model Mac RAM has been stuck at 8 GB for over a decade, breaking Apple's historical two-year upgrade cadence
On-device LLM features will be RAM-hungry, making the stagnation a more pressing issue
Apple silicon efficiency may account for roughly a 2x improvement in memory performance, but that doesn't fully excuse the stagnation
All iPhone 16 models shipping with 8 GB RAM will match base-model Macs, highlighting the absurdity of the situation
Charts show that regular base RAM increases effectively stopped when Tim Cook took over as CEO
money_quotes: It has always been the case that Apple has been open to criticism for base-model RAM being "one click" too low.
It's impossible to look at Schaub's charts and not see that regular increases in base RAM effectively stopped when Tim Cook took over as CEO.
Apple silicon efficiency notwithstanding, more RAM is better, and certainly more future-proof.
It's downright bizarre to think that come this fall, all iPhone 16 models will sport as much RAM as base model Macs.
Memory is like an orgasm. It's a lot better if you don't have to fake it.
key_insight: Apple's base-model Mac RAM has stagnated at 8 GB for over a decade, and the arrival of RAM-hungry on-device AI makes this penny-pinching increasingly untenable.
